coredata.dc:title: Hydrophobic-carbon-dot-based dual-emission micelle for ratiometric fluorescence biosensing and imaging of Cu2+ in liver cells
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Linlin
Feng, Chongchong
Xu, Jie
Wang, Fengyang
Yu, Haijun
Xu, Zhiai
Zhang, Wen
coredata.dc:description: Copper is closely related to liver damage, therefore, it is essential to develop a simple and sensitive strategy to detect copper ions (Cu2+) in liver cells. A hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs)-based dual-emission fluorescent probe for Cu2+ was prepared by encapsulating HCDs in micelles formed by self-assembly of amphiphilic polymer DSPE-PEG and tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP)-modified DSPE-PEG. The obtained probe showed characteristic fluorescence emissions of HCDs and TCPP with large emission shift of 170nm with single-wavelength excitation. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of TCPP was quenched and that of HCDs remained unchanged, displaying ratiometric fluorescence response to Cu2+. The developed probe exhibited high sensitivity (detection limit down to 36nM) and selectivity to Cu2+ over other substances, and the probe was used to image the changes of Cu2+ level in liver cells successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophobic carbon dots
Ratiometric
Micelle
Fluorescence
Copper ion
TCPP

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence responses of the probe to Cu2+
Imaging of Cu2+ in liver cells
Design of the HCDs-based micelle for ratiometric fluorescence Cu2+ sensing
Characterization of HCDs and HCDs-loaded micelle
Live cell imaging of Cu2+ with the probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the prepared HCDs. Inset was the HRTEM image. (B) The particle size distribution histograms. (C) Absorption spectra of the HCDs in chloroform. (D) Fluorescence spectra of the HCDs at different excitation wavelengths in chloroform.
(A) TEM image of the probe. (B) Size distribution of the probe in PB (pH=7.4).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (black line), the probe (red line) and DSPE-PEG-TCPP (blue line). (B) Fluorescence spectra of different volume of HCDs (A425nm=0.34) encapsulated in micelles. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the probe with different concentration ratio of DSPE-PEG and DSPE-PEG-TCPP. (D) The fluorescence stability of the probe in PB with different pH. The concentrations of the probe was 0.1mg/mL. Excitation wavelength, 405nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) Fluorescence spectra change of the dual-mission probe upon the exposure to different concentration of Cu2+. (B) Linear fitting curve of FTCPP/FHCD versus different concentration of Cu2+. (C) Fluorescence responses of the ratiometric probe towards various metal ions. The red bars represent the addition of metal ions (200mM for Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+; 50μM for other cations). The black bars represent the subsequent addition of 50μM Cu2+ ions to the solution containing other metal ions. (D) Fluorescence responses of the ratiometric probe towards various amino acids. The red bars represent the addition of 100μM amino acids. The black bars represent the subsequent addition of 50μM Cu2+ ions to the solution containing other amino acids or thiol compounds. The concentrations of the probe was 0.1mg/mL. Excitation wavelength, 405nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

CLSM images of HL-7702 cells (A) and HepG2 cells (B). Scale bar=25µm. The concentrations of the probe incubated with cells was 0.1mg/mL. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

CLSM images of live HL-7702 cells before (A) and after (B, C) treatment with 10−6 M Cu2+(B) and 10−5 M Cu2+(C). Scale bar=25µm. The concentrations of the probe incubated with cells was 0.1mg/mL. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
The chemical structures of DSPE-PEG, DSPE-PEG-TCPP and TCPP; The schematic illustration of HCDs-loaded micelle preparation and application for ratiometric Cu2+ sensing.

head.ce:title: Hydrophobic-carbon-dot-based dual-emission micelle for ratiometric fluorescence biosensing and imaging of Cu2+ in liver cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China Normal University, Shanghai 200241, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Pharmaceutics, Shanghai Institute of Materia Medica, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201203, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Pharmaceutics, Shanghai Institute of Materia Medica, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper is closely related to liver damage, therefore, it is essential to develop a simple and sensitive strategy to detect copper ions (Cu2+) in liver cells. A hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs)-based dual-emission fluorescent probe for Cu2+ was prepared by encapsulating HCDs in micelles formed by self-assembly of amphiphilic polymer DSPE-PEG and tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP)-modified DSPE-PEG. The obtained probe showed characteristic fluorescence emissions of HCDs and TCPP with large emission shift of 170nm with single-wavelength excitation. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of TCPP was quenched and that of HCDs remained unchanged, displaying ratiometric fluorescence response to Cu2+. The developed probe exhibited high sensitivity (detection limit down to 36nM) and selectivity to Cu2+ over other substances, and the probe was used to image the changes of Cu2+ level in liver cells successfully.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is a trace but vital heavy metal element which is the third abundant in organism, playing an important role in many physiological and pathological events as a cofactor of numerous enzymes (Shao et al., 2013; Fu et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2014). Abnormity of copper homeostasis may cause various diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease and Wilson disease (Kim et al., 2008; Taki et al., 2010; Tian et al., 2013). Besides, liver failure may be caused by the excessive accumulation of copper in the liver when a large mount of copper is taken into the body (Li et al., 2012; Jung et al., 2009; Zhou et al., 2008). Thus, it is worth devoting considerable efforts to develop high-performance strategies for copper ion (Cu2+) detection (Fan et al., 2013; Zong et al., 2011). Fluorescent methods have attracted great interest for determination of Cu2+ due to the superiority of biosensing and imaging in live cells and bodies (Tian et al., 2013). However, probe concentration, excitation intensity, and emission collection efficiency may affect the accuracy of fluorescent methods based on single-emission signal (Huang et al., 2014). In contrast, the ratiometric fluorescent method is less susceptible to these environmental effects (Han et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2014). While most of the ratiometric probes showed small emission shifts, which were disadvantageous for fluorescence intensity and ratio detection owing to the substantial spectral overlap (Dong et al., 2016). Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop ratiometric probes with large emission shift for the detection of Cu2+(Hou et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2012).

Carbon dots (CDs), a recent rising star of carbon materials, open a new field for fluorescence biosensing and imaging. CDs exhibit the advantages such as tiny size, broad excitation, size-tunable emission and superior photostability (Huang et al., 2012). In addition, compared to quantum dots (QDs) that usually have cytotoxicity due to the involvement of heavy metal, CDs are much safer and can be ideal candidates for biological applications (Anilkumar et al., 2011; Cao et al., 2012). Particularly, the raw materials for the synthesis of CDs are easily attainable (Tao et al., 2012). However, it is complicated to modify the surface of CDs for further application because not all of the CDs have reactive groups on the surface (Zheng et al., 2015). Besides, covalent binding is the usual method to tether functional molecules onto the hydrophilic CDs surface, which may greatly compromise the fluorescence properties of CDs. And the subsequent binding to target may also have influence on CDs fluorescence. Hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs) encapsulated by amphiphilic polymers through a simple self-assembly procedure may provide an alternative interesting method.

In this report, we developed a micelle-based nanoprobe to detect Cu2+ ratiometrically by dual-emission fluorescence with single-wavelength excitation. Polymeric micelles was selected to construct the nanoprobe due to the benefits of facile preparation, good water solubility and stability. HCDs were synthesized and encapsulated in micelles formed by self-assembly of amphiphilic polymer 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy(polyethylene glycol)-2000] (DSPE-PEG) and tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP)-modified DSPE-PEG (DSPE-PEG-TCPP). The biocompatibility and solubility of HCDs in aqueous buffer was improved without great influence on its fluorescence property (Bhunia and Jana, 2011; Lemon et al., 2015). The fluorescence of HCDs embedded in micelles were inert to Cu2+, thus acting as reference signal for offering built-in correction to avoid the effects of the environmental fluctuation. TCPP is a porphyrin derivative which is a class of conjugate macrocyclic compounds with near-red fluorescence emission and has been utilized in various applications such as catalysis, photodynamic therapy, and sensors (Choi et al., 2004). Meanwhile, TCPP was sensitive and selective to Cu2+ and its fluorescence can be quenched by Cu2+ due to the coordination between copper and the nitrogen of porphin ring. Upon excitation by single-wavelength, the probe showed dual emission peaks at 485nm and 655nm, respectively. With the addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity ratio of TCPP and HCDs (FTCPP/FHCD) changed in a concentration range of 10−7 to 10−5 M, indicating it was a highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Cu2+. In addition, the feasibility of this nanoprobe for Cu2+ determination in HL-7702 and HepG2 cells were also investigated.

Octadecylamine, 1-octadecene, ammonium pyrrolidinedithiocarbamate (PDTC) were all purchased form Aladdin reagent company (Shanghai, China). Glucose anhydrous, CuSO4·5H2O were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP) was purchased from TCI Development Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 1-Hydroxybenzotriazole anhydrous (98%, HOBT), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (99%, EDCI) and methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) were all purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd (Beijing, China). 1,2-Distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy(polyethylene glycol)-2000] (DSPE-PEG) and 1, 2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[amino (polyethylene glycol)-2000] (DSPE-PEG-NH2) was purchased from Shanghai advanced vehicle technology pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Water (18.2MΩcm) from laboratory water purification system (Hitech Instruments Co., Ltd) was used throughout the experiment.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA). The fluorescence spectra were measured with a Cary Eclipse spectrofluorophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were collected with JEM-2011 electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) were performed on Malvern Zetasizer Nanoseries (Malvern, England). HNMR of DSPE-PEG-TCPP was measured by using a Bruker-400MHz NMR spectrometer at 25°C.

HCDs were synthesized according to a reported method (Bhunia et al., 2013). Briefly, 80mg glucose, 0.8g octadecylamine and 5mL octadecene were mixed and stirred at 160°C for 30min. Then, after being cooled to room temperature, the reaction mixture turned into brown solid and the solid was dissolved by chloroform. Organic solvent was used to extract the HCDs and remove the insoluble octadecylamine. Next, we used column chromatography to further purify the hydrophobic HCDs. In this separation process, we chose silica gel (300–400 mesh) as the stationary phase with dichloromethane and methanol as the mobile phase. The HCDs dissolved in chloroform were stored at 4°C until use.

To prepare DSPE-PEG-TCPP，TCPP was dissolved in DMF and activated by EDCI and HOBT for 2h (TCPP:EDCI:HOBT=1:1.2:1.2, mol/mol/mol). Next, DSPE-PEG-NH2 dissolved in DMF was added slowly to the activated TCPP (DSPE-PEG-NH2:TCPP=1:3, mol/mol) at 0°C, then the mixture was reacted at room temperature for 24h with stirring. Following that, the mixture was dialysed extensively against distilled water to remove all impurities (dialysis bag M
w cutoff 1000Da) for 3 days and then lyophilized.

Briefly, 5mg DSPE-PEG and 0.025mg DSPE-PEG-TCPP were dissolved in 5mL chloroform respectively. HCDs was diluted to the concentration of which the absorbance at 425nm is 0.34. Then, 500μL DSPE-PEG (1mg/mL), 60μL DSPE-PEG-TCPP (0.005mg/mL) and 150μL HCDs (A425nm=0.34) were mixed in a round bottom flask, followed by slow evaporation of chloroform. 500μL phosphate buffer (PB, 0.01M, pH 7.4) was added to the round bottom flask under sonication until the solution became clear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of the probe to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence detection was all performed in phosphate buffer (PB, 0.01M, pH 7.4). Typically, various concentrations of Cu2+ were directly added to the solution containing the probe at room temperature for 10min, then the fluorescence spectra were collected on a Cary Eclipse spectrofluorophotometer with excitation of 405nm.

HepG2 cells were cultured in MEM-ALPHA medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin, and were grown in a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37°C. HL-7702 cells were cultured in RPMI medium (1640) supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin, and were grown in a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37°C. HL-7702 cells (1×105 cell/mL) were harvested from flasks and plated in a 96-well microliter plate with 100μL per well. Plate was incubated at 37°C for 24h to allow the cells to attach. Then, the culture media was removed and the cells were incubated in culture medium containing the probe with different concentrations (0, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10mg/mL) for 48h. MTT solution (10μL, 5mg/mL) was then added, followed by incubating for 4h to form formazan dye. Finally, the supernatant was removed before 150μL of DMSO was added to each well, and the plate was shaken for 10min to dissolve the formazan. Absorbance was measured three times at 540nm in a Multiskan MK3. The number of viable cells was determined according to the following formula: cell viability (%)=the absorbance of experimental group / the absorbance of blank control group ×100% (Fu et al., 2013; Zhuang et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of Cu2+ in liver cells
ce:section.ce:para: First, one day before fluorescence imaging experiments, HL-7702 cells and HepG2 cells were plated on 35-mm Petri dish with 14-mm bottom well in culture medium in a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37°C, respectively. Then, the probe was added into the cells and incubated for 4h. Next, to examine whether the probe could visualize changes of fluorescence intensity ratios in HL-7702 cells treated with exogenous Cu2+, after washing with PBS three times to remove the extracellular probe, the exogenous Cu2+ with different concentrations and 100μM PDTC were added directly to the Petri dish and incubated for 40min. Confocal fluorescence imaging including XY-scan and spectrum-scan was performed with a Leica TCS-SP8 confocal scanning microscope. Excitation of cells loaded with the probes was carried out with a 405nm diode laser, and emissions were then collected simultaneously for the two channels in the 450–550nm and 600–700nm range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the HCDs-based micelle for ratiometric fluorescence Cu2+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation and sensing mechanism of the ratiometric probe was shown in Scheme 1. Firstly, amphiphilic polymer DSPE-PEG was modified with TCPP. TCPP is a porphyrin derivative which has been utilized in various applications such as catalysis and photodynamic therapy. TCPP has strong near-red fluorescence emission with peak centered at 655nm. Secondly, HCDs were prepared with a facile hydrothermal method and wrapped by amphiphilic polymer DSPE-PEG and DSPE-PEG-TCPP to form polymeric nanoparticle. Cu2+ can selectively quench the red fluorescence of TCPP due to the coordination between copper and the nitrogen on porphin ring, while the fluorescence emission of the HCDs embedded in micelles remained constant, thus providing a reliable reference signal for ratiometric detection of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of HCDs and HCDs-loaded micelle
ce:section.ce:para: HCDs was synthesized from glucose according to a green method to provide the reference fluorescence signal (Bhunia et al., 2013). The formation of HCDs was firstly confirmed by TEM measurements. The as-prepared HCDs were well-dispersed in chloroform ( Fig. 1A) and had a size distribution from 2.3 to 4.3nm with an average diameter of 3.5nm (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in inset of Fig. 1A displayed well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21nm corresponding to the (100) diffraction facet of graphite carbon. The absorption spectra was shown in Fig. 1C. It can be observed that there was an absorption peak at about 425nm. Fig. 1D was the fluorescence spectra of HCDs at different excitation wavelengths. When excited at wavelengths in the range of 385–565nm, the emission peak gradually red-shifted along with the increase of the excitation wavelength. And the maximum emission intensity was observed upon excitation at 425nm, which was consistent with the result of absorption spectra. Using quinine sulfate as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the HCDs was calculated to be around 5.8%. The photostability of the HCDs and TCPP were tested and shown in Fig. S1, respectively. When excited at 405nm, their emissions keep almost unchanged over time, suggesting the good photostability of the HCDs and TCPP.

The amphiphilic polymer DSPE-PEG-TCPP was synthesized and the chemical composition of the obtained polymers was studied by 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S2), and the results suggested the successful synthesis of DSPE-PEG-TCPP.

The hydrophobic HCDs were transformed to be water-soluble through a self-assembly method using amphiphilic polymer DSPE-PEG and DSPE-PEG-TCPP (Bhunia and Jana, 2011; Pellegrino et al., 2004). The TEM image showed that the average diameter of the HCDs-based micelle was about 40nm ( Fig. 2A). The size of micelle was further confirmed by DLS (Fig. 2B) and the result was consistent with that of TEM.

The fluorescence spectra of the HCDs, DSPE-PEG-TCPP and the probe excited at 405nm were shown in Fig. 3A. Compared with the black curve (HCDs) and the blue curve (DSPE-PEG-TCPP), the red curve (probe) showed both emissions of HCDs and TCPP with maximum at 485nm and 655nm, respectively, demonstrating that the probe was successfully prepared. Notably, the large separation of two emission peaks from HCDs and TCPP (170nm) may be favorable to ensure the accurate evaluation of emission intensities and ratios for Cu2+ assay.

The amount of the HCDs encapsulated in the micelle was optimized. As demonstrated in Fig. 3B, a gradual increase of the HCDs fluorescence intensity was observed along with increasing of the HCDs volume (A425nm=0.34) from 50μL to 150μL. The fluorescence intensity of HCDs encapsulated in the micelle enhanced no longer along with further increase of the HCDs amount used for micelle preparation. Therefore, 150μL HCDs (A425nm=0.34) was the encapsulation capacity limit in the case of 500μL DSPE-PEG (1mg/mL) and 60μL DSPE-PEG-TCPP (0.005mg/mL) used. Thus, 150μL HCDs was chosen as the optimum volume.

The fluorescence intensity of TCPP changed with the variation of concentration ratio of DSPE-PEG and DSPE-PEG-TCPP used to prepare the probe. Then, we added different volume of DSPE-PEG-TCPP while adding the same amount of DSPE-PEG and HCDs to optimize fluorescence property. As shown in Fig. 3C, if we continued to increase the concentration ratio, the fluorescence intensity of TCPP wound increase further and the FTCPP/FHCD ratio would increase accordingly. The goal of this work was to apply the nanoprobe to detect and image Cu2+ in live cells ratiometrically with single-wavelength excitation. Therefore, the intensity of TCPP and HCD channel should not be greatly different. Finally, we chose 10,000:6 as the optimum concentration ratio and the dual-emission fluorescence intensity ratio (FTCPP/FHCD) equal to 3.5 approximately.

In order to explore the biological applications of the nanoprobe, it is essential to discuss the influence of the broad biologically relevant pH to the probe. As shown in Fig. 3D, the probe signals were independent of phosphate buffer's pH value in the range from 4 to 9, which illustrated that the probe had good stability in different pH environment and can be used for biological analysis.

Good photostability and colloidal stability were significant for biomedical application of nanoparticles in real samples. Thus, the photostability and colloidal stability of the micelle were tested and shown in Figs. S1C and S3. The dual-emissions of the micelle kept almost unchanged over 1h scanning time (Fig. S1C). The micelle showed good dispersity in either serum-free or serum-containing PB over 48h, implying good colloidal stability as confirmed by dynamic light scattering measurement.

We then discussed the potential of the probe for Cu2+ assay. The response of the dual-emission fluorescent probe toward different concentrations of Cu2+ was investigated. The fluorescence of TCPP was used as response signal for Cu2+ due to the efficient coordination between them, while the fluorescence of HCDs encapsulated in the micelle served as the reference signal. As demonstrated in Fig. 4A, upon addition of Cu2+, the red fluorescence (FTCPP: 655nm) continuously decreased, while no obvious variations were obtained at the emission peak centered at 485nm from HCDs. Meanwhile, the dual-emission fluorescence intensity ratio (FTCPP/FHCD) decreased linearly with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 2×10−7 to 1×10−6 M [Y=3.83-2.73×(R2=0.9946)] (Fig. 4B). The detection limit was calculated to be around 36nM (based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3). Compared with literatures, the ratiometric probe exhibited comparable or higher sensitivity and low detection limit (Table S1). In addition, we used the developed nanoprobe to detect Cu2+ in river water sample by performing standard addition experiments and acceptable recoveries were obtained (Table S2), indicating that the probe was promising in real sample analysis.

The selectivity investigation were carried out because the complex environment of intracellular system presents a great challenge for the detection of Cu2+. Thus, the interferences from metal ions, amino acids, and other biological species were examined. First, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the probe were studied after their exposure to other metal ions (including Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Hg2+ and Cu+) with the same detection condition as that of the Cu2+. The red bars in Fig. 4C showed that the ratio of FTCPP/FHCD except in the case of Cu+ didn’t change obviously compared to the dual-mission probe, which suggested the good selectivity of the probe to Cu2+ over other metal ions. Then, considering that amino acids have the capabilities of interacting with a lot of metal ions, the fluorescence intensity ratio was also examined after their exposure to amino acids including tryptophan (Trp), phenylalanine (Phe), arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met), alanine (Ala), asparagine (Asn), glutamic acid (Glu), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), valine (Val), cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) under the same conditions as that of Cu2+(the red bars in Fig. 4D). As shown in the black bars in Fig. 4C and D, further addition of Cu2+ to the probe containing other metal ions or amino acids, the ratios of FTCPP/FHCD all decreased drastically, which indicated that probe could meet the selective requirements for physiological application. All the results demonstrated the high specificity of the ratiometric probe for Cu2+ biosensing in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live cell imaging of Cu2+ with the probe
ce:section.ce:para: There were reports that hepatotoxicity during the development of liver injury was closely related to the level of copper and the concentration of Cu2+ in human hepatoma cells was different with that of human normal liver cells (Li et al., 2012). First of all, we explored the ability of the ratiometric probe for live cell imaging in live human normal liver cells (HL-7702) and human liver carcinoma cells (HepG2). Taking into account that toxicity is also very essential for further application in a biological system, MTT assay was carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the probe to HL-7702 cells. In the presence of the probe with the concentration from 0 to 0.10mg/mL, the cellular viabilities were estimated to be greater than 90% (Fig. S4) after incubation for 48h. This result confirmed its low cytotoxicity and implied its potential for imaging applications.

Live cell imaging in HL-7702 and HepG2 cells was conducted by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). Cells were incubated with the probe in culture medium for 4h at 37°C before imaging. As shown in Fig. 5A, it was observed that the green and red channels both displayed bright fluorescence signals, which proved the ability of the probe to enter into live cells and maintained its dual-emission characteristic. The FTCPP/FHCD ratio was estimated to be 3.7 (Fig. S5A). For HepG2 cells, the green and red channels were also clearly observed with fluorescence intensity from the red channel slightly differed. The FTCPP/FHCD ratio was estimated to be 2.8 (Fig. S5A). This meant that our results were consistent with that previously reported (Li et al., 2012).

Next, HL-7702 cells were used as the model system to show the capability of the probe to image Cu2+ concentration change in live cells. Upon addition of exogenous Cu2+ to HL-7702 cells, the fluorescence intensity from red channel decreased drastically while the fluorescence intensity from green channel changed barely ( Fig. 6B and C). Accordingly, the FTCPP/FHCD decreased from 3.7 to 1.2 and 0.5 with the extra addition of 1μM and 10μM Cu2+ (Fig. S5B), which was consistent with the responses of the probe to Cu2+ in PB buffer. These results provided clear evidence that the dual-mission probe could be applied to explore exogenous Cu2+-based response and imaging in liver cells.

In summary, a HCD-based ratiometric micelle for the detection of Cu2+ has been developed for biosensing and imaging of Cu2+ in liver cells by using the fluorescence of TCPP as the response signal and the fluorescence of HCDs as the reference signal. The prepared probe displayed excellent properties of dual-emission by single-wavelength excitation, good selectivity, low detection limit and high sensitivity. Meanwhile, the probe with small size showed low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility and good cell permeability. Accordingly, the fluorescent nanoprobe has been applied for imaging of Cu2+ successfully, providing a method to monitor the changes of Cu2+ level in liver cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675055, 21305047, 21375040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.01.066.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
